reasoning: The informal statement says "exactly one" of x² - py² = 2 or x² - py² = -2 has an integral solution. Due to Lean 4 operator precedence (∧ at 35 binds tighter than ∨ at 30), the Lean expression `A ∨ B ∧ ¬(A ∧ B)` parses as `A ∨ (B ∧ ¬(A ∧ B))`, which is logically equivalent to `A ∨ B` — i.e., "at least one has a solution". This is weaker than "exactly one has a solution": it is true even when both have solutions, whereas the informal claim asserts they cannot both have solutions. The intended encoding would need parenthesization like `((∃...=2) ∨ (∃...=-2)) ∧ ¬((∃...=2) ∧ (∃...=-2))`. Since the informal claim implies the Lean claim but not vice versa, the Lean formalization is strictly weaker.
